title: Application Partitioning

Application Partitioning

Application Partitioning

The possibilities for partitioning are determined when the system and software architecture are defined. A crucial prerequisite for partitioning is that all components represent the same type of functionality, such as presentation logic, application logic, transaction handling, background processing, and generating reports.

The components must also have a well-balanced level of granularity, that is, they should not be too large or too small. If they are too large, with a lot of functionality, they cannot be partitioned. If they are too small, they require too much intercomponent processing for the system to handle efficiently.

Uniface enables you to partition files of various formats, such as compiled code and database files. You can assign components to execute remotely on the most appropriate server. Services, reports, and 3GL services are eligible for remote execution, whereas forms always run on a client. The execution paths are defined using assignment files.

The Uniface Router and the Uniface Server enable transparent deployment. The Uniface Router handles communication, while Uniface Servers handle:

  • Requests for files and data residing on remote servers. The files include compiled forms, services, and reports. Client processes and other Application Server processes might issue these requests.
  • Processing of services and reports on remote machines
  • Execution of 3GL components

A Uniface Server can be shared, processing requests from multiple clients, or it can be exclusive, having a connection to only one client. Shared servers use resources more efficiently and can handle a greater load, whereas exclusive servers provide better performance.

Both shared servers and exclusive servers handle synchronous and asynchronous requests from client processes. However, routing asynchronous requests to a shared server is preferable, because these requests are not performance-sensitive. To enhance sharing, use stateless activate requests whenever possible. Stateful component requests need to go back to the same server. If this happens frequently, contention problems can occur.

To fine-tune the application during deployment, you need to verify whether the design supports application partitioning using the Uniface Server.

Transactions on Shared Servers

By default, only one database transaction is created in a shared server process. This means that if a remote instance of one client performs a store and commit or a rollback, all outstanding changes are committed or discarded from remote instances of other clients, and any incomplete hitlist is truncated. It is therefore advisable to use stateless transactions when using remote database transaction on a shared server.

It is also possible to prevent this problem by using TRANSACTION=TRUE when creating a remote instance on a shared server. This causes each client to get its own (encapsulated) transaction within a shared server process. This may cause delays if one client's commit transaction takes a long time, thereby blocking other clients from committing.

Related Links
  • Uniface Server

    A Uniface Server is a server-based process that handles requests for remote data and component execution that it receives from client applications.

  • Uniface Router

    The Uniface Router is a multi-threaded process (urouter) responsible for interprocess communication in Uniface applications. It listens for requests from Uniface client applications and routes them to the appropriate Uniface Server, and it passes messages between various Uniface applications.
